1: <header>Environmental impact and efficiency of use of resources of different mountain dairy farming systems </header>

M. Berton, G. Bittante, F. Zendri, M. Ramanzin, S. Schiavon, E. Sturaro Department of Agronomy, Food, Natural resources, Animals and Environment, University of Padova, Viale dell'Università 16, 35020 Legnaro, Padova, Italy

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Dairy farming systems Life cycle assessment Mountain areas Sustainability|In the European Alps traditional, low-input dairy farming systems still coexist with modern high-input intensive systems. This study aimed at evaluating the effect of different Alpine farming systems on the environmental footprint, production efficiency (gross energy conversion ratio, ECR) and competition between feed and food (potentially human-edible gross energy conversion ratio, HeECR). Data originated from 37 dairy farms located in the Trento province (eastern Italian Alps), from which four dairy systems were derived by performing non- hierarchical cluster analysis based on farm facilities and management features (traditional, either with tie or loose stalls, and intensive, either with or without use of silages, systems). Environmental footprint was computed using a cradle-to-farm gate Life Cycle Assessment model. One kg fat- and protein-corrected Milk (FPCM) and 1 m² of agricultural land were used as functional units. Global warming (GWP), acidification (AP) and eu- trophication (EP) potentials, cumulative energy demand (CED) and land occupation (LO) were included as impact categories. System boundaries included herd and manure management, on-farm feedstuffs production and purchased feedstuffs and materials. Mean impact values per 1 kg FPCM were 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq (GWP), 21.1 + 4.3 g SO2-eq. (AP), 6.3 + 1.2 g PO4-eq. (EP), 5.0 + 2.0 MJ (CED), 1.4 + 0.5 m²/y (LO), whereas per 1 m2 were 0.8 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq (GWP), 16.3 + 4.2 g SO2-eq. (AP), 4.9 + 1.3 g PO4-eq. (EP), 3.8 + 1.8 MJ (CED). Mean ECR was 5.17 + 0.89 MJ/MJ, with 88% of gross energy provided by non-human edible feedstuffs. A large variability was found both between and within dairy systems, in terms of environmental footprint and production efficiency. Impact values were slightly greater per unit of product and lower per unit of area in traditional than in intensive farms, although generally without significant differences. Production efficiency of traditional farms was 17% lower in terms of ECR but 59% greater in terms of HeECR, due to a lower proportion of purchased concentrates in animal rations, with a positive contribution to food balance and diet self-suffi- ciency. These results indicate that the transition from traditional towards intensive systems improved only slightly the environmental footprint of dairy farming, but increased markedly its dependence on external con- centrate feeds and the feed-food competition. In perspective, different aspects of mountain dairy systems, such as the conversion into food of human non edible feeds, the low impacts at the local scale, the ability to conserve grasslands under a land-sharing perspective, and in general the associated ecosystem services, should be con- sidered when aiming to improve their environmental sustainability.
2: During the last decades, European dairy milk farming systems have experienced a massive intensification aiming to increase milk produc- tion and feed efficiency in terms of nutrient output per unit of nutrient input (Oenema et al., 2014). This trend has influenced also the dairy systems located in mountainous areas, such as in the European Alps, traditionally characterized by small-scale farms whose dairy cows are fed rations based on farm-produced forage (Battaglini et al., 2014). In fact, the sector has undergone notable changes in the last decades, with

a severe decline in the numbers of both animals and farms (in the Ita- lian Alps: - 29% and - 60%, respectively, from 1990 to 2010: ISTAT, 2010), and a locally more or less pronounced trend towards the sub- stitution of traditional dual-purpose breeds with specialized dairy breeds and the use of rations richer in purchased concentrates, which has led to a weakening of the traditional, strong relationship between livestock and local forage resources (ISTAT, 2010; Battaglini et al., 2014). As a result of these changes, a great variability in dairy farming systems can be identified in the Alps, where traditional, low-input and low-productivity farms coexist with intensive, high-input and high-
Fluxes of nitrogen and phosphorous from and to
Fluxes of output (milk and animals) outside the system
Fig. 1. System boundaries of Life Cycle Assessment model.
productivity farms (Sturaro et al., 2009; Hörtenhuber et al., 2010; Sturaro et al., 2013).

Such variability should also translate into different environmental performances of dairy systems. In this respect, it has been demonstrated that an increase in milk productivity has a positive effect on the pro- duction efficiency (i.e., feed conversion ratio) and a mitigating effect on the environmental impact related to the production of 1 kg of milk (Gerber et al., 2011; Lorenz et al., 2019), which should favor intensive farms. However, when comparing different dairy systems, it is funda- mental to consider the agro-ecosystems that they typically manage (Nemecek and Alig, 2016). In mountain areas, traditional grassland- based livestock systems provide multiple ecosystem services (Rodríguez-Ortega et al., 2014; Faccioni et al., 2019), and the evalua- tion of environmental impact based only on the functional unit of milk cannot capture the multiple relations of Alpine livestock systems with their land. In addition, mountain areas, especially those higher in al- titude, have a low or nil suitability for arable crops. Therefore, animal production in these areas has to be based on ruminant systems, such as dairy, which are able to use the local non-human edible feed resources. The production of animal food using non-human edible resources is an important issue, because of the increasing concern about the potential competition between feed and food (i.e., the use of potentially human- edible feeds for animal feeding; Ertl et al., 2015). The few studies to our knowledge available on the environmental impact of Alpine dairy farming systems (Hörtenhuber et al., 2010; Guerci et al., 2014; Kiefer et al., 2015; Salvador et al., 2016) have applied the Life Cycle Assess- ment (LCA, ISO, 2006) method, which aims at evaluating the impacts of a product following its life cycle. Among these studies, only Hörtenhuber et al., 2010 and Guerci et al., 2014 specifically compared different dairy systems in terms of environmental impact, which was computed only in terms of greenhouse gases emissions.

Thus, the aim of this study was to compare how Alpine dairy farming systems different in terms of input/output intensity and use of grasslands could differ also in environmental impact per unit of milk and unit of area, in production efficiency and the competition between feed and food produced by such systems. The analysis considers as a case study a large sample of mixed breed dairy farms located in the Trento province (eastern Italian Alps), where a variety of different farming systems, from traditional to intensive, coexist (Sturaro et al., 2013).

7: The study was conducted in the autonomous Province of Trento, located in north-eastern Italy, in the middle of the Alps. The surface extension of the province is nearly 6000 km2 and elevation ranges from 66 to 3769 m asl. The utilized agricultural area (UAA) covers nearly one-fifth of the province, and is mainly managed as meadows and pastures (81% of UAA) (ISTAT, Istituto Nazionale di Statistica, 2010). Dairy farming has a predominant role in the local agro-livestock sys- items, with farms mainly associated to cooperative dairies that produce typical and Protected Designation of Origin (PDO) cheeses (mainly "Trentingrana"), Bittante et al., 2011a and b;). This study involved 37 mixed breed dairy farms, representative of the local dairy sector variability in terms of herd size, management (feeding system, facilities, equipment, ...) and reared cattle breeds (Sturaro et al., 2013).
2.2. Goal and scope definition The parameters for the application of the assessment model, the

data collection and editing and the computation of the impact in- dicators were performed following the LCA scheme as proposed in the ILCD Handbook (European Commission, 2010). The dairy farm was taken as the reference unit for the cradle-to-farm-gate LCA model. Within each dairy farm, the functional unit (FU) was the amount of desirable output which the total impact has to be referred to (ISO, 2006). Because sampled farms destined milk essentially to cheese production, whose final characteristics could be affected by milk che- mical composition (Bittante et al., 2011b), 1 kg of fat- and protein- corrected milk (FPCM) was adopted as functional unit. The milk was corrected for its fat and protein content to standards of 4.0% and 3.3% for fat and protein, respectively, according to Gerber et al. (2010) (see Supplementary Table 1 for details). However, as the use of different functional units could alter LCA modeling results (Ross et al., 2017), 1 m² of agricultural area was also adopted as an alternative FU. The system boundaries (Fig. 1) were set in order to include the impacts occurring on-farm (herd handling, manure management, and feedstuffs production) as well as those related to the background systems for the production and use of the farm agricultural inputs, purchased feed- stuffs, fuel, electricity and bedding materials. The impact categories assessed were: global warming (GWP), acidification (AP) and eu- trophication (EP) potentials, cumulative energy demand (CED), and land occupation (LO). The impact caused by inputs derived from multi- functional systems was allocated to the reference unit according to an economic allocation method (de Vries and de Boer, 2010). The alloca- tion of the whole impact between milk and body weight gain (BWG) derived by cull cows and calves sold to the market was based on IDF (2015) method. The Allocation Factor (AF) for milk was calculated as follows: AF = 1-6.04 X R, where R = MBWG Mmilk, and MBWG is the sum of BWG of all animals sold whereas Mmilk is the sum of milk sold corrected for fat and protein as described above.

9: A one-year period was taken as time reference for the data collec- tion. The detailed list of information collected is reported in the Supplementary materials (Supplementary Table 1). Each dairy farm was visited at least two times by the same operator during the year. To have a comprehensive view of the structure, management and pro- duction of each farm, data collected on farm were complemented with data obtained from the milk recording system of the Breeder association of Trento Province and from the milk quality payment records of the cooperative dairies. Regarding the farm structure, information about the manure management system (solid VS slurry) and the type of stalls (tie or loose) was recorded during the farm visits, whereas farm agri- cultural area (FAA) was collected from official farms' crop registers.

Data about herd management and animal production were com- puted as average of monthly or individual records (Supplementary Table 1). Number of dairy cows (lactating and dry), age at first calving, number of lactations, days in milk, dry period and calving interval were recorded from farm documents and the milk recording system. The number of calves and heifers in a farm was modelled on the basis of the number of dairy cows, number of lactations and calving interval. Herd size in terms of Livestock Units (LU) was computed on the basis of the conversion factors provided by European Union (animal older than 2 years: 1 LU, heifer for replacement: 0.6 LU). The measure of chest girth and body condition score (BCS) was performed once on all the dairy cows in the farm by the same skilled technician. The estimation of body weight (BW) was based on the method developed by Gallo et al. (2017) for a farm sample studied in the same area, which took into account the breed of the dairy cows as well. The estimation of the BW of the cow at the first calving was based on NRC (2001) (Supplementary Table 2).
Milk and animals (calves sold and culled cows) were included into the farm animal production. Mean milk yield per dairy cow and per

year was computed as the average of the milk production records col- lected from the milk recording system (10-11 records per cow per year), and milk yield per LU and per ha of FAA were computed as the ratio between the farm milk production per year (milk yield per lac- tating cow and per year multiplied by number of lactating cows) and the total LU and FAA. Mean protein and fat milk content per farm were computed as average of data obtained from the milk payment system records (22-25 analyses per farm per year). Regarding the animal surplus sold to the market, number of surplus calves was obtained as total calves born minus female calves retained for replacement, whereas the number of culled cows was computed as 1/number of lacta- tions*number of dairy cows.

The feeding management system was recorded during the farm visits, as well as the ration composition (proportion of ingredients) as yearly average for lactating cows, dry cows and heifers. The method for the estimation of dry matter intake (DMI) differed on the basis of the feeding system, i.e. total mixed ratio (TMR) VS traditional individual feeding administration. We assumed that the limiting factor for milk production was energy, because we based this assumption on the facts that in literature dietary protein contents lower than 120 g/kg had limited influence on the dry matter intake of lactating cows (Schiavon et al., 2015), and that very few farms in the current research had a dietary protein content lower than 120 g/kg. For the TMR group, the estimated DMI was computed as the ratio between the net energy (NE) requirements of the cow and the net energy per 1 kg DM of diet. For estimating NE requirements, the procedure described in NRC (2001) was followed (for details, see Supplementary Tables 2a, 2b and 3). The net energy of 1 kg DM of diet was computed as the total NE available in the diet offered (sum of the quantity of each feed multiplied by NE/kg DM of each feed) divided for the quantity (as DM) of the diet ad- ministered (Supplementary Table 4). For the second group, i.e. with traditional feeding, the daily individual intake of concentrates and compound feeds was recorded, but it was not possible to measure the intake of forages. So, the NE of forages was calculated as the difference between the total NE required by the average animal and the NE sup- plied by the other feeds. This procedure was used for lactating cows, dry cows and heifers. The chemical composition, gross energy and NE content of all feeds except compound feeds were derived from the lit- erature and previous studies conducted in the study area (Schiavon et al., 2019). For compound feeds, the values declared in the com- mercial labels were used. Nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) input-output flows were computed according to the procedure suggested by Ketelaars and Van der Meer (1999) on the basis of the requirement derived from NRC (2001) (see Supplementary Tables 5). N excretion was calculated as the difference between N intake from the diet and N retention for growth, pregnancy, and milk. This procedure was used for lactating cows, dry cows and heifers.

About the farm production inputs, the FAA management (land area per crop, type and quantity of fertilizers and pesticides for agricultural production) was derived from official farms' crop registers, whereas farm invoices were used to record the consumption of electricity, fuel (diesel and petrol), methane and LPG gases and of the bedding mate- rials (straw and sawdust). Self-sufficiency of the diet was computed as the ratio between the DMI produced on-farm and the total DMI, sepa- rately for dairy cow and heifers.
10: A one-year period was taken as time reference for the data collec- tion. The detailed list of information collected is reported in the Supplementary materials (Supplementary Table 1). Each dairy farm was visited at least two times by the same operator during the year. To have a comprehensive view of the structure, management and pro- duction of each farm, data collected on farm were complemented with data obtained from the milk recording system of the Breeder association of Trento Province and from the milk quality payment records of the cooperative dairies. Regarding the farm structure, information about the manure management system (solid VS slurry) and the type of stalls (tie or loose) was recorded during the farm visits, whereas farm agri- cultural area (FAA) was collected from official farms' crop registers.

Data about herd management and animal production were com- puted as average of monthly or individual records (Supplementary Table 1). Number of dairy cows (lactating and dry), age at first calving, number of lactations, days in milk, dry period and calving interval were recorded from farm documents and the milk recording system. The number of calves and heifers in a farm was modelled on the basis of the number of dairy cows, number of lactations and calving interval. Herd size in terms of Livestock Units (LU) was computed on the basis of the conversion factors provided by European Union (animal older than 2 years: 1 LU, heifer for replacement: 0.6 LU). The measure of chest girth and body condition score (BCS) was performed once on all the dairy cows in the farm by the same skilled technician. The estimation of body weight (BW) was based on the method developed by Gallo et al. (2017) for a farm sample studied in the same area, which took into account the breed of the dairy cows as well. The estimation of the BW of the cow at the first calving was based on NRC (2001) (Supplementary Table 2).
Milk and animals (calves sold and culled cows) were included into the farm animal production. Mean milk yield per dairy cow and per

year was computed as the average of the milk production records col- lected from the milk recording system (10-11 records per cow per year), and milk yield per LU and per ha of FAA were computed as the ratio between the farm milk production per year (milk yield per lac- tating cow and per year multiplied by number of lactating cows) and the total LU and FAA. Mean protein and fat milk content per farm were computed as average of data obtained from the milk payment system records (22-25 analyses per farm per year). Regarding the animal surplus sold to the market, number of surplus calves was obtained as total calves born minus female calves retained for replacement, whereas the number of culled cows was computed as 1/number of lacta- tions*number of dairy cows.

The feeding management system was recorded during the farm visits, as well as the ration composition (proportion of ingredients) as yearly average for lactating cows, dry cows and heifers. The method for the estimation of dry matter intake (DMI) differed on the basis of the feeding system, i.e. total mixed ratio (TMR) VS traditional individual feeding administration. We assumed that the limiting factor for milk production was energy, because we based this assumption on the facts that in literature dietary protein contents lower than 120 g/kg had limited influence on the dry matter intake of lactating cows (Schiavon et al., 2015), and that very few farms in the current research had a dietary protein content lower than 120 g/kg. For the TMR group, the estimated DMI was computed as the ratio between the net energy (NE) requirements of the cow and the net energy per 1 kg DM of diet. For estimating NE requirements, the procedure described in NRC (2001) was followed (for details, see Supplementary Tables 2a, 2b and 3). The net energy of 1 kg DM of diet was computed as the total NE available in the diet offered (sum of the quantity of each feed multiplied by NE/kg DM of each feed) divided for the quantity (as DM) of the diet ad- ministered (Supplementary Table 4). For the second group, i.e. with traditional feeding, the daily individual intake of concentrates and compound feeds was recorded, but it was not possible to measure the intake of forages. So, the NE of forages was calculated as the difference between the total NE required by the average animal and the NE sup- plied by the other feeds. This procedure was used for lactating cows, dry cows and heifers. The chemical composition, gross energy and NE content of all feeds except compound feeds were derived from the lit- erature and previous studies conducted in the study area (Schiavon et al., 2019). For compound feeds, the values declared in the com- mercial labels were used. Nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) input-output flows were computed according to the procedure suggested by Ketelaars and Van der Meer (1999) on the basis of the requirement derived from NRC (2001) (see Supplementary Tables 5). N excretion was calculated as the difference between N intake from the diet and N retention for growth, pregnancy, and milk. This procedure was used for lactating cows, dry cows and heifers.

About the farm production inputs, the FAA management (land area per crop, type and quantity of fertilizers and pesticides for agricultural production) was derived from official farms' crop registers, whereas farm invoices were used to record the consumption of electricity, fuel (diesel and petrol), methane and LPG gases and of the bedding mate- rials (straw and sawdust). Self-sufficiency of the diet was computed as the ratio between the DMI produced on-farm and the total DMI, sepa- rately for dairy cow and heifers.

14: <header>2.3.2. Computation of impacts and life cycle impact assessment </header>

The methods for the computation of the on-farm emissions included in GWP, AP and EP are reported in Supplementary Tables 6a, 6b and 7. The on-farm GWP emissions included those related to the enteric me- thane (CH4), to CH4 and nitrous oxide (N20) released during the manure management and storage, and to N2O volatilization at the field level after fertilizers application. The computation of the enteric CH4 was based on the equation suggested by Ramin and Huhtanen (2013), based on DMI and chemical composition of rations (content in neutral detergent fibre, non-structural carbohydrate, ether extract, organic
15: Characteristics of the clusters obtained from the sample of 37 Alpine dairy farms (Trento Province, North East Italy). Percent values are in brackets.

Variable|Cluster|Cluster|Cluster|Cluster Variable|Traditional farms|Traditional farms|Intensive farms|Intensive farms Variable|tie stall|loose stall|with silage|no silage Farms, N|9|8|8|12 Type of stalls|Type of stalls|Type of stalls|Type of stalls|Type of stalls Tie, N|9 (100)|1(13)|3 (38)|0 (0) Loose, N|0 (0)|7 (88)|5 (63)|12 (100) Feeding system|Feeding system|Feeding system|Feeding system|Feeding system Traditional, N|7 (78)|6 (75)|1 (12)|5 (42) TMR, N|2 (22)|2 (25)|7 (88)|7 (58) Use of silages|Use of silages|Use of silages|Use of silages|Use of silages Yes|0 (0)|0 (0)|8 (100)|0 (0) No|9 (100)|8 (100)|0 (0)|12 (100) Use of inputs (Mean + SD)|Use of inputs (Mean + SD)|Use of inputs (Mean + SD)|Use of inputs (Mean + SD)|Use of inputs (Mean + SD) Concentrates, kg DM/|4.9 + 1.7|4.1 2.2|7.4 + 3.0|9.0 + 1.4 cow/d|cow/d|cow/d|cow/d|cow/d Farm agricultural area, ha/cow|0.5 + 0.2|0.9 + 0.2|0.6 + 0.2|0.4 + 0.1
Values are reported as frequencies in table, and as percent values in italics between brackets.

matter intake), and the computation of CH4 and N2O due to manure management and the fertilizers application at the field was based on the IPCC (2006). The emissions considered for AP were those related to the volatilization of N in the forms of ammonia (NH3) and nitrogen oxides (NOX) that occur during the manure storage and the fertilizers appli- cation at the field. The default emission factors proposed by IPCC (2006) were used to estimate the NH3 and NO emissions. Regarding EP, it was assumed that 30% of the N from fertilizers (either organic or mineral) was lost in the form of nitrate (NO3) through leaching (IPCC, 2006). The method of Nemecek and Kägi (2007) was used to estimate the phosphorus loss (in the form of phosphate PO43- The impact factor (IF) related to the agricultural inputs for the on-farm feedstuffs production (fertilizers, pesticides, seeds), to the bedding materials (straw and sawdust) and to the gas used for the farm management were derived from Ecoinvent (Ecoinvent Centre, 2014) database. Fuel and electricity production impacts related to GWP were taken into account using IFs provided by the European Environmental Agency (EEA, 2013) and the Italian Environmental Agency (ISPRA, 2011) reports, respec- tively, whereas the IFs related to the other impact categories were de- rived from the Ecoinvent database. The quantity of each substance's emission related to GWP, AP and EP were converted to the common unit of the impact categories to which it contributed by using the same procedure adopted by Berton et al. (2017).

Cumulative energy demand was related to the energy (from non- renewable and renewable sources) needed for the farm management and included the production and use of the energy sources directly used in the farm (fuel, gas, electricity) and of those indirectly used in the production of the off-farm inputs. All the IFs were derived from Ecoinvent database. Land occupation was related to the surface area needed to produce the inputs utilized in the farm and was the sum of the farm agricultural area (data collected during the farm visits) and the off-farm area destined to the production of the off-farm inputs (data derived from Ecoinvent database).

The quantity of each substance's emission related to GWP, AP and EP were converted to the common unit of the impact categories to which it contributed by using the same procedure adopted by Berton et al. (2017).

Cumulative energy demand was related to the energy (from non- renewable and renewable sources) needed for the farm management and included the production and use of the energy sources directly used in the farm (fuel, gas, electricity) and of those indirectly used in the production of the off-farm inputs. All the IFs were derived from Ecoinvent database. Land occupation was related to the surface area needed to produce the inputs utilized in the farm and was the sum of the farm agricultural area (data collected during the farm visits) and the off-farm area destined to the production of the off-farm inputs (data derived from Ecoinvent database).

19: The efficiency of the production system to transform energy con- tained in the feedstuffs fed to cattle into energy contained in the milk was evaluated on the basis of gross energy conversion ratio (ECR), computed as the gross energy in the feedstuffs divided by the gross energy of FPCM. In order to take into account the diversion of

potentially human-edible feedstuffs from humans to animals, we as- sessed the potentially human-edible gross energy conversion ratio (HeECR) computed as the ratio between the potentially human-edible gross energy in the feedstuffs and the gross energy in FPCM (Wilkinson, 2011). The potentially human-edible share of the ration was computed with the procedure suggested by Wilkinson (2011). The energy content of FPCM (3.15 MJ/kg) was derived from IDF (2015).
20: <header>2.5. Identification of the dairy farming systems and statistical analysis </header>

The identification of the different dairy farming systems was based on Sturaro et al. (2013). A cluster analysis was performed using the SAS FASTCLUS procedure (SAS, 2013). The cluster criteria were chosen in order to reflect the structure, the management and the input intensity of the farms as well as to exclude all the variables regarding the animals and the farm output. Five cluster criteria were chosen, three concerning the farm structure/management - type of stalls (tie stall VS loose stall), feeding system (TMR vs traditional), use of silages (yes VS no) - and two concerning the input intensity - amount of concentrates into the diet of dairy cows (kg DM/dairy cow/day) and quantity of FAA available for each dairy cow (ha/dairy cow). The number of clusters was optimized on the basis of the cubic clustering criterion indicator.

The contribution of each production stage to each impact category was evaluated through a hotspot analysis (European Commission, 2010). The farm and diet characteristics, the impact categories and ECR and HeECR values were analyzed with a general linear model (PROC GLM, SAS, 2013), which included the fixed effect of the cluster. Dif- ferences between Least Squares means were orthogonally contrasted: traditional farms VS intensive farms, within traditional farms (tie stalls VS loose stalls) and within intensive farms (with silages VS without si- lages).
21: <header>2.5. Identification of the dairy farming systems and statistical analysis </header>

The identification of the different dairy farming systems was based on Sturaro et al. (2013). A cluster analysis was performed using the SAS FASTCLUS procedure (SAS, 2013). The cluster criteria were chosen in order to reflect the structure, the management and the input intensity of the farms as well as to exclude all the variables regarding the animals and the farm output. Five cluster criteria were chosen, three concerning the farm structure/management - type of stalls (tie stall VS loose stall), feeding system (TMR vs traditional), use of silages (yes VS no) - and two concerning the input intensity - amount of concentrates into the diet of dairy cows (kg DM/dairy cow/day) and quantity of FAA available for each dairy cow (ha/dairy cow). The number of clusters was optimized on the basis of the cubic clustering criterion indicator.

The contribution of each production stage to each impact category was evaluated through a hotspot analysis (European Commission, 2010). The farm and diet characteristics, the impact categories and ECR and HeECR values were analyzed with a general linear model (PROC GLM, SAS, 2013), which included the fixed effect of the cluster. Dif- ferences between Least Squares means were orthogonally contrasted: traditional farms VS intensive farms, within traditional farms (tie stalls VS loose stalls) and within intensive farms (with silages VS without si- lages).
22: Four clusters were obtained from the cluster analysis of the farms sampled. The characteristics regarding the cluster criteria per cluster are reported in Table 1. The farms were nearly homogenously dis- tributed over the four clusters. Clusters 1 and 2 grouped farms with a traditional feeding system (78 and 75%, respectively), no use of silages and low level of concentrates, but with different type of stalls (tie stalls in Cluster 1 and loose stall in Cluster 2). On the other hand, clusters 3 and 4 grouped farms with a widespread use of TMR and high levels of concentrates for dairy cows (7.4 and 9.0 kg/d, respectively), and dif- fered in the use of silages, adopted in cluster 3 but not in cluster 4. Consequently, the four clusters were organized in two groups of tradi- tional farms: tie-stalls traditional dairy farming system (cluster 1), loose-stalls traditional dairy farming system (cluster 2); and in two groups of intensive farms: intensive dairy farming system with silages (cluster 3) and no-silages intensive dairy farming system (cluster 4).

Table 2 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the farm and herd size and characteristics. The mean altitude of the traditional (grouping clusters 1 and 2) and the intensive (clusters 3 and 4) dairy farming systems was 786 and 706 m a.s.l., re- spectively. The traditional dairy farming systems were characterized by a lower FAA (-32%), a greater share of FAA managed as grassland 18%, P < .05) and fewer animals (nearly a half in terms of LU), but a similar replacement rate (33% VS 35%), as compared to the intensive dairy farming systems. Moreover, the traditional dairy farming systems maintained herds much less specialized towards milk production, where the specialized-breeds (Holstein Friesian and Brown Swiss) re- presented nearly 60% of the total dairy cows on average, in contrast with 90% in the intensive dairy farming systems. About the input uti- lization, the yearly use of farm energy sources (fuel, LPG, electricity) and bedding materials did not show significant differences between traditional and intensive systems. P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar. nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, *** P < .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 Livestock unit: defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 4 HF: Holstein Friesian, BS: Brown Swiss, AG: Alpine Grey, RE: Rendena, 5 ep: equivalent of petrol (11).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar. Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 FPCM: fat- and protein-corrected milk. 4 LU: livestock unit, defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 5 FAA: farm agricultural area, 6 BCS: body condition score, 7 BWG: body weight gain.

nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar. nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 FPCM: fat- and protein-corrected milk. 4 LU: livestock unit, defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 5 FAA: farm agricultural area, 6 BCS: body condition score, 7 BWG: body weight gain.

nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar. Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 FPCM: fat- and protein-corrected milk. 4 LU: livestock unit, defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 5 FAA: farm agricultural area, 6 BCS: body condition score, 7 BWG: body weight gain.

nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar. Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 FPCM: fat- and protein-corrected milk. 4 LU: livestock unit, defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 5 FAA: farm agricultural area, 6 BCS: body condition score, 7 BWG: body weight gain.

nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, *** P < .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 Livestock unit: defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 4 HF: Holstein Friesian, BS: Brown Swiss, AG: Alpine Grey, RE: Rendena, 5 ep: equivalent of petrol (11).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar.
