Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 FPCM: fat- and protein-corrected milk. 4 LU: livestock unit, defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 5 FAA: farm agricultural area, 6 BCS: body condition score, 7 BWG: body weight gain.

nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar. nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 FPCM: fat- and protein-corrected milk. 4 LU: livestock unit, defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 5 FAA: farm agricultural area, 6 BCS: body condition score, 7 BWG: body weight gain.

nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar. Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 FPCM: fat- and protein-corrected milk. 4 LU: livestock unit, defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 5 FAA: farm agricultural area, 6 BCS: body condition score, 7 BWG: body weight gain.

nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar. Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error. 3 FPCM: fat- and protein-corrected milk. 4 LU: livestock unit, defined following the EU livestock schemes (cattle > 2 years = 1 LU, cattle 6 months to 2 years = 0.6 LU), 5 FAA: farm agricultural area, 6 BCS: body condition score, 7 BWG: body weight gain.

nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

P < .05), either from manure or from chemical fertilizers. Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms were located at a significantly higher altitude (905 VS 667 m a.s.l) and presented a lower FAA (14 vs 32 ha), managed less nitrogen per unit of FAA and used a lower amount of bedding sawdust per LU than loose-stalls farms. Within intensive dairy farming systems, farms using silages were located at a higher, although not significantly, altitude (744 vs 668 m a.s.l.) and had significantly greater FAA 83%, P < .05), lower FAA managed as grassland, and a lower total N application per unit of FAA -21%), but depended more strongly on chemical fertilizers (11% vs 0% of total N applied, P < .01) than the no-silages farms.

Table 3 illustrates the results (LSmeans and F-values) of the ortho- gonal contrasts for the milk and beef productions and reproduction traits. The traditional dairy farming systems produced significantly less milk than the intensive ones, either per lactating cow and LU (ap- proximately - 35% of FPCM/year, P < .001), or per ha of FAA 41%, P < .001). The traditional dairy farming systems did not differ from the intensive ones in terms of mean lactation number, lactation length, days in milk, calving interval and days open, but had leaner ( 8%, P < .001) and older 13%, P < .01) dairy cows, which had the first calving at a higher age (35 VS 31 months). No significant dif- ferences between traditional and intensive dairy farming systems were observed in BWG, with traditional systems showing generally lower values of BWG productivity than intensive ones.
Within the traditional dairy farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed

lower values of milk productivity per lactating cow and LU 10% and - 6%, respectively), although the differences were not significant, and greater per ha FAA 105%, P < .05) than loose-stalls farms. The reproductive traits values between tie- and loose-stalls were similar, with significant differences only for the age at first calving and the length of dry period -11% and + 17% for tie-stalls in comparison to loose-stalls, respectively, P < .05). Within the intensive dairy farming systems, milk productivity difference between farms with and without silages was small -4% and - 3%, respectively) when expressed per lactating cow or LU, whereas farms with silages showed a significantly lower milk productivity per ha FAA -31%, P < .05). Differences in the reproductive traits and BWG productivity were slight and not sig- nificant (from - 12% to + 16%), except for BWG productivity per unit of FAA, with significantly lower value showed by farms with silages ( -39%, P < .05).

Table 4 reports the results about the diet characteristics for dairy cows (as mean value between lactating and dry cows, weighed on the relative number of lactating and dry cows) and heifers per dairy farming systems. Dairy cows herded according to traditional dairy farming systems showed a lower DMI 12%, P < .001), with a ration characterized by a significantly greater share of forages (70% vs 56%, P < .001) and a significantly lower energy content -4% as gross energy and - 12% as net energy, P < .01) than those herded in the intensive systems, whereas crude protein and phosphorous content were similar. nitrogen ingestion, secretion, and excretion, with dairy cows herded in the traditional systems showing a N excretion 12% (P < .05) lower than those herded in the intensive systems. Although significant dif- ferences in phosphorous intake and retention were observed, traditional and intensive systems did not differ significantly in phosphorous ex- cretion, although the relative difference was similar to that of N ex- cretion (-15% for traditional with respect to intensive systems). Be- sides, the traditional systems relied less on purchased feedstuffs than the intensive farming systems (+41% in terms of self-sufficiency of the diet, P < .01). Within traditional systems, no significant differences in the diet characteristics were observed between tie-stalls or loose-stalls farms, with the exception of enteric CH4 per 1 kg of diet, which was slightly higher -4%, P < .05) in the tie-stall group. Within the in- tensive systems, the farms using silages fed their cows a diet poorer in crude protein content (-10%, P < .05) than the no-silages farms, which resulted into a lower N excretion -15%, P < .05). These diets were also characterized by a greater proportion of feedstuffs produced on farm (+44%, P < .05).

With heifers, differences between farming systems in feed intake, diet characteristics and nutrient (nitrogen and phosphorous) excretion were negligible. The average dry matter intake of a replacement heifer ranged from 5.4 to 5.8 kg DM/d, with a crude protein content of 12.3-13.1%.

The impact categories mean values and the contribution of each production stage to each impact category are illustrated in Fig. 2. The production of 1 kg FPCM as average of all the dairy systems of the study area emitted nearly 1.0 + 0.3 kg CO2-eq, demanding 5.0 + 2.0 MJ of cumulative energy and occupying nearly 1.4 + 0.5 of agri- cultural land. On-farm production stages contributed for more than

50% of the total impact for all the impact categories with the exception of CED (nearly 30%). In particular, CH4 released during enteric fer- mentation was the main contributor to GWP, followed by the produc- tion of the off-farm feedstuffs, whereas manure management and on- farm feedstuffs were the main contributors to AP and EP, and the latter also to LO.

No significant differences were observed between intensive and traditional farming systems for all the impact categories expressed per 1 kg FPCM (Table 5), with the exception of LO, which was significantly greater in traditional farming systems with respect to intensive systems +29%, P < .01). Within traditional systems, farms with tie-stalls had a significantly lower LO than loose farms -31%, P < .01), whereas within intensive systems small and not significant differences were observed between farms with silages and no-silages farms (differences ranged from 8% to + 9%, depending on the impact category). When the impact categories results (per 1 kg FPCM) were disaggregated for each production stage (Supplementary Table 8), traditional dairy farming system evidenced greater values for all the production stages in all the impact categories, with the exception of off-farm feedstuffs production (GWP, EP, CED, LO). When the impact was reported per 1 m² of LO, no significant differences were found between traditional and intensive farming systems. Within traditional systems, tie-stalls farms showed significantly greater values for GWP, EP and CED per 1 m² respect to those with loose animals (differences ranged from + 19% to 44%, according to the impact category) whereas differences within intensive systems between farms using or not using silages were small and not significant (from - 11% to 4%,).

significantly lower footprint -34% and - 32%, respectively for AP and EP, P < .05). Within the two traditional and intensive farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed a greater value of EP than loose-stalls farms and farms using silages showed lower values of AP and EP than no-silages farms. On the contrary, the AP and EP impact related to the nutrients pressure on the off-farm unit area did not show differences between traditional and intensive farming systems, whereas, within traditional ones, tie-stalls farms showed off-farm EP values greater than those related to loose-stalls farms and within intensive systems off-farm AP and EP values were greater for the farms using silages than for no- silages farms.

The gross energy conversion ratios (ECR and HeECR) were both significantly affected by farm clusters (P < .01). In particular, the traditional farming system was significantly less efficient in whole gross energy conversion (5.63 VS 4.81 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk), but more efficient when only the potentially human-edible portion was considered (0.35 VS 0.85 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk). Within tra- ditional farms, tie-stalls farms had significantly lower (more favorable) values of ECR than loose-stalls farms, but similar values of HeECR. Small and no significant differences in terms of ECR and HeECR were observed between silages farms and no-silages farms.

1 F values refer to the orthogonal contrasts: traditional vs intensive farms, within traditional farms: tie-stalls vs loose-stalls farms, within intensive farms: with silages VS without silage). Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, *** P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error, 3 Standard dairy cow: average dairy cow on 365 days, considering a lactation period and the dry period, 4 Forages: ratio between forages and silages and total DMI. 5 Self-sufficiency of diet: ratio between dry matter intake from feed produced on farm and total dry matter intake.

significantly lower footprint -34% and - 32%, respectively for AP and EP, P < .05). Within the two traditional and intensive farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed a greater value of EP than loose-stalls farms and farms using silages showed lower values of AP and EP than no-silages farms. On the contrary, the AP and EP impact related to the nutrients pressure on the off-farm unit area did not show differences between traditional and intensive farming systems, whereas, within traditional ones, tie-stalls farms showed off-farm EP values greater than those related to loose-stalls farms and within intensive systems off-farm AP and EP values were greater for the farms using silages than for no- silages farms.

The gross energy conversion ratios (ECR and HeECR) were both significantly affected by farm clusters (P < .01). In particular, the traditional farming system was significantly less efficient in whole gross energy conversion (5.63 VS 4.81 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk), but more efficient when only the potentially human-edible portion was considered (0.35 VS 0.85 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk). Within tra- ditional farms, tie-stalls farms had significantly lower (more favorable) values of ECR than loose-stalls farms, but similar values of HeECR. Small and no significant differences in terms of ECR and HeECR were observed between silages farms and no-silages farms.

1 F values refer to the orthogonal contrasts: traditional vs intensive farms, within traditional farms: tie-stalls vs loose-stalls farms, within intensive farms: with silages VS without silage). Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, *** P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error, 3 Standard dairy cow: average dairy cow on 365 days, considering a lactation period and the dry period, 4 Forages: ratio between forages and silages and total DMI. 5 Self-sufficiency of diet: ratio between dry matter intake from feed produced on farm and total dry matter intake.

significantly lower footprint -34% and - 32%, respectively for AP and EP, P < .05). Within the two traditional and intensive farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed a greater value of EP than loose-stalls farms and farms using silages showed lower values of AP and EP than no-silages farms. On the contrary, the AP and EP impact related to the nutrients pressure on the off-farm unit area did not show differences between traditional and intensive farming systems, whereas, within traditional ones, tie-stalls farms showed off-farm EP values greater than those related to loose-stalls farms and within intensive systems off-farm AP and EP values were greater for the farms using silages than for no- silages farms.

The gross energy conversion ratios (ECR and HeECR) were both significantly affected by farm clusters (P < .01). In particular, the traditional farming system was significantly less efficient in whole gross energy conversion (5.63 VS 4.81 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk), but more efficient when only the potentially human-edible portion was considered (0.35 VS 0.85 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk). Within tra- ditional farms, tie-stalls farms had significantly lower (more favorable) values of ECR than loose-stalls farms, but similar values of HeECR. Small and no significant differences in terms of ECR and HeECR were observed between silages farms and no-silages farms.

Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, *** P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error, 3 Standard dairy cow: average dairy cow on 365 days, considering a lactation period and the dry period, 4 Forages: ratio between forages and silages and total DMI. 5 Self-sufficiency of diet: ratio between dry matter intake from feed produced on farm and total dry matter intake.

significantly lower footprint -34% and - 32%, respectively for AP and EP, P < .05). Within the two traditional and intensive farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed a greater value of EP than loose-stalls farms and farms using silages showed lower values of AP and EP than no-silages farms. On the contrary, the AP and EP impact related to the nutrients pressure on the off-farm unit area did not show differences between traditional and intensive farming systems, whereas, within traditional ones, tie-stalls farms showed off-farm EP values greater than those related to loose-stalls farms and within intensive systems off-farm AP and EP values were greater for the farms using silages than for no- silages farms.

The gross energy conversion ratios (ECR and HeECR) were both significantly affected by farm clusters (P < .01). In particular, the traditional farming system was significantly less efficient in whole gross energy conversion (5.63 VS 4.81 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk), but more efficient when only the potentially human-edible portion was considered (0.35 VS 0.85 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk). Within tra- ditional farms, tie-stalls farms had significantly lower (more favorable) values of ECR than loose-stalls farms, but similar values of HeECR. Small and no significant differences in terms of ECR and HeECR were observed between silages farms and no-silages farms.

1 F values refer to the orthogonal contrasts: traditional vs intensive farms, within traditional farms: tie-stalls vs loose-stalls farms, within intensive farms: with silages VS without silage). Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, *** P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error, 3 Standard dairy cow: average dairy cow on 365 days, considering a lactation period and the dry period, 4 Forages: ratio between forages and silages and total DMI. 5 Self-sufficiency of diet: ratio between dry matter intake from feed produced on farm and total dry matter intake.

significantly lower footprint -34% and - 32%, respectively for AP and EP, P < .05). Within the two traditional and intensive farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed a greater value of EP than loose-stalls farms and farms using silages showed lower values of AP and EP than no-silages farms. On the contrary, the AP and EP impact related to the nutrients pressure on the off-farm unit area did not show differences between traditional and intensive farming systems, whereas, within traditional ones, tie-stalls farms showed off-farm EP values greater than those related to loose-stalls farms and within intensive systems off-farm AP and EP values were greater for the farms using silages than for no- silages farms.

The gross energy conversion ratios (ECR and HeECR) were both significantly affected by farm clusters (P < .01). In particular, the traditional farming system was significantly less efficient in whole gross energy conversion (5.63 VS 4.81 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk), but more efficient when only the potentially human-edible portion was considered (0.35 VS 0.85 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk). Within tra- ditional farms, tie-stalls farms had significantly lower (more favorable) values of ECR than loose-stalls farms, but similar values of HeECR. Small and no significant differences in terms of ECR and HeECR were observed between silages farms and no-silages farms.

1 F values refer to the orthogonal contrasts: traditional vs intensive farms, within traditional farms: tie-stalls vs loose-stalls farms, within intensive farms: with silages VS without silage). Significance: * P < .05, ** P < .01, *** P .001, 2 MSE: mean square error, 3 Standard dairy cow: average dairy cow on 365 days, considering a lactation period and the dry period, 4 Forages: ratio between forages and silages and total DMI. 5 Self-sufficiency of diet: ratio between dry matter intake from feed produced on farm and total dry matter intake.

significantly lower footprint -34% and - 32%, respectively for AP and EP, P < .05). Within the two traditional and intensive farming systems, tie-stalls farms showed a greater value of EP than loose-stalls farms and farms using silages showed lower values of AP and EP than no-silages farms. On the contrary, the AP and EP impact related to the nutrients pressure on the off-farm unit area did not show differences between traditional and intensive farming systems, whereas, within traditional ones, tie-stalls farms showed off-farm EP values greater than those related to loose-stalls farms and within intensive systems off-farm AP and EP values were greater for the farms using silages than for no- silages farms.

The gross energy conversion ratios (ECR and HeECR) were both significantly affected by farm clusters (P < .01). In particular, the traditional farming system was significantly less efficient in whole gross energy conversion (5.63 VS 4.81 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk), but more efficient when only the potentially human-edible portion was considered (0.35 VS 0.85 MJ feedstuffs per 1 MJ in milk). Within tra- ditional farms, tie-stalls farms had significantly lower (more favorable) values of ECR than loose-stalls farms, but similar values of HeECR. Small and no significant differences in terms of ECR and HeECR were observed between silages farms and no-silages farms.

65: The distribution of farm structure and management types and of input investment levels found in this study shows a differentiation of farm strategies that ranged from traditional low-input to modern high- input farms. Similar results were found in previous studies performed in this study area of the Italian eastern Alps (Sturaro et al., 2009; Sturaro et al., 2013) and in other regions of the Alpine system (Hörtenhuber et al., 2010). The effect of this differentiation of farm strategies and investments on output level, efficiency, and environmental footprint will be discussed in the next paragraphs.
4.1. Identified Alpine dairy farming systems: relation between inputs, productivity and efficiency

The cluster analysis first of all was able to distinguish the "tradi- tional" from the "intensive" farming systems, which differed markedly for facilities, farm and herd management and organization. In general, there was a great variability either between these two dairy farming systems or within them. Nevertheless, within traditional farms, those with tie-stalls were separated from those with loose-stalls, and within intensive farms, those using silages were separated from those not using silages. The greater presence of loose-stalls farms using TMR, silages and high level of concentrates in the intensive farming systems high- lights a greater level of investments, since these features imply eco- nomic expense to adapt facilities and management. Beyond the need for new, modern facilities, these investments seem to be based on a dif- ferent genetics, with the substitution of traditional Alpine breeds (Simmental, and other local dual purpose breeds) with the Holstein Friesian. Moreover, intensive dairy farming systems show a stronger production focus in dairy cow management (anticipation of first cal- ving, lower dairy cows mean age, shorter dry period) as well as feeding management (TMR richer in concentrates and in crude protein and energetic starch, ether extracts nutrients). As these investments are technically and financially demanding for the farmer, the research of the possible size economies is fundamental to properly obtain the marginal gains in production and efficiency: in this sense, larger FAA and herd size of the intensive farms are a probable consequence of the exploitation of those size economies Weersink and Tauer (1991).

The milk productivity is the first indicator of the investments' results in dairy farms: in this study, intensive farming systems actually showed a greater milk productivity per cow than traditional farms (see Table 3). This large effect is the combined result of using more specialized dairy breeds and of properly supporting their nutrient and environmental needs. All the farms were mixed in terms of dairy cows' breed, with different proportions between specialized and local breeds among dairy farming systems. In particular, tie-stalls traditional farms showed a great relative presence of local breeds (40%), a combination that could affect productivity, feeding regime and diet composition in this dairy farming system. Disentangling the genetic from management factors is not easy, but an analysis that we have carried out on the same group of farms quantified that the differences among different breeds in the quantitative issues of dairy production are much lower after correcting for farm intensity (Stocco et al., 2017a). On the contrary, qualitative and technological differences among milks of different breeds are much less affected by management aspects (Stocco et al., 2017b).

Moreover, this productivity gain in the intensive farms was obtained with a parallel increase in efficiency production in terms of unit of agricultural land occupied and in terms of ECR (Table 5). Land is a limited resource in quantitative and qualitative terms (Pimentel and Pimentel, 1996) and its productive use can be in competition with natural biodiversity (Haines-Young, 2009). Consequently, the efficiency in land occupation allows to leave more land for the maintenance of natural habitats. In the same way, feedstuffs (derived from the pro- ductive use of the land) and the relative energy are a limited resource, which needs to be utilized with highest efficiency. Intensive farms

showed a lower ECR value than traditional ones, because of the greater use of concentrates in the dairy cow diets, which resulted into more energy-concentrated diets which could satisfy requirements of more productive cows with a similar level of DMI (e.g., Mayne and Gordon, 1984). However, in recent years the debate over efficiency use of land and feedstuffs energy has been enriched with new insights that ques- tioned the general term "efficiency use". About land use, the "sparing- or-sharing" debate, about whether is more effective in minimizing human impact the adoption of biodiversity-friendly farming systems to maximize biodiversity conservation and food production on the same land or the adoption of highly-intensive farming systems to minimize human land occupation boosting natural ecosystems maintenance (Phalan et al., 2011), resulted into contrasting evidences, because of a complex matrix of advantages and disadvantages of each mitigation measure (Dudley and Alexander, 2017). On the other hand, concentrate feeds are, in large part, potentially human-edible. Consequently, an increase in production efficiency based on a greater use of concentrates could cause the diverting of feeds directly usable by humans. For in- stance, Ertl et al. (2015) reported that lowland dairy farms in Austria, while producing more milk than hilly and alpine dairy farms, showed a negative contribution to the food delivering balance. In this study, traditional farming systems, while showing worse values of ECR, were capable to obtain one MJ of edible milk using less potentially human- edible gross energy than the intensive systems. This indicates a poten- tial trade-off between milk production efficiency and potentially human-edible food delivering. In this sense, mountain traditional farming systems managed to exploit in a productive way the local forage resources, reducing the dependence on lowland concentrates, with the related economic and transporting burden. Moreover, in mountain areas, where agricultural land is mainly grassland, the deli- vering of dairy milk based on local forages can provide human-edible energy and protein in an efficient way, positively contributing to maximize land occupation efficiency in an increasing land-scarce world (van Zanten et al., 2016). Besides, within intensive farming systems, the use of silages did not affect potentially human-edible gross energy conversion ratio, probably because grass silage, totally non-human- edible, and maize silage, whose grain portion is potentially human- edible, averaged only 7% and 15% of the total animal diet (data not shown).

The effect of this differentiation of farm strategies and investments on output level, efficiency, and environmental footprint will be discussed in the next paragraphs.
4.1. Identified Alpine dairy farming systems: relation between inputs, productivity and efficiency

The cluster analysis first of all was able to distinguish the "tradi- tional" from the "intensive" farming systems, which differed markedly for facilities, farm and herd management and organization. In general, there was a great variability either between these two dairy farming systems or within them. Nevertheless, within traditional farms, those with tie-stalls were separated from those with loose-stalls, and within intensive farms, those using silages were separated from those not using silages. The greater presence of loose-stalls farms using TMR, silages and high level of concentrates in the intensive farming systems high- lights a greater level of investments, since these features imply eco- nomic expense to adapt facilities and management. Beyond the need for new, modern facilities, these investments seem to be based on a dif- ferent genetics, with the substitution of traditional Alpine breeds (Simmental, and other local dual purpose breeds) with the Holstein Friesian. Moreover, intensive dairy farming systems show a stronger production focus in dairy cow management (anticipation of first cal- ving, lower dairy cows mean age, shorter dry period) as well as feeding management (TMR richer in concentrates and in crude protein and energetic starch, ether extracts nutrients). As these investments are technically and financially demanding for the farmer, the research of the possible size economies is fundamental to properly obtain the marginal gains in production and efficiency: in this sense, larger FAA and herd size of the intensive farms are a probable consequence of the exploitation of those size economies Weersink and Tauer (1991).

The milk productivity is the first indicator of the investments' results in dairy farms: in this study, intensive farming systems actually showed a greater milk productivity per cow than traditional farms (see Table 3). This large effect is the combined result of using more specialized dairy breeds and of properly supporting their nutrient and environmental needs. All the farms were mixed in terms of dairy cows' breed, with different proportions between specialized and local breeds among dairy farming systems. In particular, tie-stalls traditional farms showed a great relative presence of local breeds (40%), a combination that could affect productivity, feeding regime and diet composition in this dairy farming system. Disentangling the genetic from management factors is not easy, but an analysis that we have carried out on the same group of farms quantified that the differences among different breeds in the quantitative issues of dairy production are much lower after correcting for farm intensity (Stocco et al., 2017a). On the contrary, qualitative and technological differences among milks of different breeds are much less affected by management aspects (Stocco et al., 2017b).

Moreover, this productivity gain in the intensive farms was obtained with a parallel increase in efficiency production in terms of unit of agricultural land occupied and in terms of ECR (Table 5). Land is a limited resource in quantitative and qualitative terms (Pimentel and Pimentel, 1996) and its productive use can be in competition with natural biodiversity (Haines-Young, 2009). Consequently, the efficiency in land occupation allows to leave more land for the maintenance of natural habitats. In the same way, feedstuffs (derived from the pro- ductive use of the land) and the relative energy are a limited resource, which needs to be utilized with highest efficiency. Intensive farms

showed a lower ECR value than traditional ones, because of the greater use of concentrates in the dairy cow diets, which resulted into more energy-concentrated diets which could satisfy requirements of more productive cows with a similar level of DMI (e.g., Mayne and Gordon, 1984). However, in recent years the debate over efficiency use of land and feedstuffs energy has been enriched with new insights that ques- tioned the general term "efficiency use". About land use, the "sparing- or-sharing" debate, about whether is more effective in minimizing human impact the adoption of biodiversity-friendly farming systems to maximize biodiversity conservation and food production on the same land or the adoption of highly-intensive farming systems to minimize human land occupation boosting natural ecosystems maintenance (Phalan et al., 2011), resulted into contrasting evidences, because of a complex matrix of advantages and disadvantages of each mitigation measure (Dudley and Alexander, 2017). On the other hand, concentrate feeds are, in large part, potentially human-edible. Consequently, an increase in production efficiency based on a greater use of concentrates could cause the diverting of feeds directly usable by humans. For in- stance, Ertl et al. (2015) reported that lowland dairy farms in Austria, while producing more milk than hilly and alpine dairy farms, showed a negative contribution to the food delivering balance. In this study, traditional farming systems, while showing worse values of ECR, were capable to obtain one MJ of edible milk using less potentially human- edible gross energy than the intensive systems. This indicates a poten- tial trade-off between milk production efficiency and potentially human-edible food delivering. In this sense, mountain traditional farming systems managed to exploit in a productive way the local forage resources, reducing the dependence on lowland concentrates, with the related economic and transporting burden. Moreover, in mountain areas, where agricultural land is mainly grassland, the deli- vering of dairy milk based on local forages can provide human-edible energy and protein in an efficient way, positively contributing to maximize land occupation efficiency in an increasing land-scarce world (van Zanten et al., 2016). Besides, within intensive farming systems, the use of silages did not affect potentially human-edible gross energy conversion ratio, probably because grass silage, totally non-human- edible, and maize silage, whose grain portion is potentially human- edible, averaged only 7% and 15% of the total animal diet (data not shown).

70: <header>4.2. Environmental footprint: dairy farming systems and different functional units </header>

Various studies have evaluated the environmental footprint of dairy production systems, considering a variegated set of impact categories, but few studies focused on the Alpine dairy production systems in Italy (Guerci et al., 2014; Salvador et al., 2016), southern Germany (Kiefer et al., 2015) and Austria (Hörtenhuber et al., 2010). Despite the fact that the LCA models used in these studies could be slightly different, GWP values found in this study were within the range for GWP (0.90-1.72 kg CO2-eq) per 1 kg FPCM. About AP and EP, comparison could be done only with Salvador et al. (2016), with which the AP values found in this study are similar (21.2-22.3 g SO2-eq), whereas mean EP was greater (3.0-3.8 PO4-eq), whereas for CED and LO were not used as impact categories.

The results of this study showed small and often non-significant differences in the impact categories between the traditional and the intensive farming systems, and within the two groups, per 1 kg FPCM or per 1 m² of agricultural area. The small differences between traditional and intensive dairy farming system are due to a compensation among the different production stages within the impact categories, e.g. the greater enteric methane emission in traditional dairy system (in line with Bittante et al., 2018) VS the lower off-farm feedstuffs contribution to GWP as compared to the intensive dairy system.
Previous studies (Thoma et al., 2013; Lorenz et al., 2019) have found similar and non-significant impact values for different dairy

systems, although only for GWP per 1 kg of milk, thus suggesting that considering only the dairy system could be not sufficient if the aim is mitigating the dairy systems environmental footprint. This study fo- cused on mountain dairy systems, little covered by previous studies, and found a similar trend seen in Thoma et al. (2013) and Lorenz et al. (2019), not only for GWP but also for a wider set of impact categories such as AP, EP and CED, whereas traditional dairy systems need sig- nificant more land to obtain 1 kg FPCM than intensive dairy systems, as discussed in section 4.1. Moreover, the inclusion of the farm production efficiency into the assessment do not allow to determine in an univocal way which dairy farming system (traditional or intensive) had a better overall performance, with traditional dairy farming systems showing a lower ECR but a better capacity for providing human-edible products from inedible feed resources than intensive dairy farming system. In this sense, the daily management of the farmer emerges as a notable feature on which to focus the attention in order to increase the en- vironmental sustainability of Alpine dairy systems. This study does not aim to analyze specific mitigation options, but its results suggest that the mitigation to be applied should consider the variability of the production systems, between systems and within systems, taking into account the peculiarities of the Alpine mountain territory in terms of agricultural vocation. Moreover, as the practical implementation of any mitigation option can alter the characteristics of the production systems and can have indirect effects outside the production systems, e.g. other production systems, territory, local population, etc., these alterations should be taken into account in a dynamic way through con- sequential-based analysis (Finnveden et al., 2009)

Moreover, when referring the environmental impact per unit of area, the fact that this could occur in different territories (e.g. at the farm and in the off-farm areas) has to be considered, especially for those environmental phenomena characterized by a regionalized impact such as acidification and eutrophication, for which considering only the total impact value could be unfair for the local territory and population (Potting and Hauschild, 2006). For this reason, the ratios between the on-farm acidifying and eutrophying agents and the on-farm area could be more effective indicators of the effects observable in the local ter- ritory, particularly when this territory has particular characteristics and vulnerabilities such as the mountain areas. Contrary to the total AP and EP impact per unit of area, the on-farm AP and EP impact was lower for traditional than intensive farming systems, a result due to a lower burden per LU (Table 2) and a greater on-farm area per 1 kg FPCM (Supplementary Table 8). The local nutrients burden is the sum of nutrients cycling in the local territory plus the nutrients entered through chemical fertilizers use and the import of off-farm feedstuffs. In this sense, both traditional farming systems actually presented no use of chemical fertilizers and greater values of diet self-sufficiency, implying a lower import of nutrients from the off-farm territory. At the local level, diet self-sufficiency could alter not only the nutrient burden but also the farm and the territory management: a low self-sufficiency diet rate implies that farms are less dependent on the local territory. This disconnection with the mountainous territory could have negative ef- fects on the abandonment of managed meadows and related territory maintenance, grassland-related biodiversity and also landscape (Cocca et al., 2012).

74: The results of this study highlighted the great variability of facilities and management practices within the dairy systems of Trentino Province, with the presence of small, traditional and low inputs farms and of large, intensive and high input farms. This variability was re- flected by a likewise large variability in farm performances, production efficiency, competition between feed and food and environmental footprint indicators. Both traditional and intensive dairy systems showed a large variability between farms of the same group, evidencing that the farming system is not the main driver of environmental impacts

and there is room to improve the environmental sustainability by working on specific farm management practices. Moreover, the dairy farming systems based on mountain grasslands showed to be particu- larly efficient in terms of food balance, thanks to the low level of po- tentially human edible feedstuffs. These results can be used to support strategies and policies aiming at the promotion of an efficient integra- tion between dairy farming systems and mountain areas. In perspective, the evaluation of dairy farming systems in the Alps should be expanded to the economic and social pillars of sustainability and of the different ecosystem services assured by mountain ecosystems.
